title: Village Homes: A Radical Plan
location: UC Davis Design Museum, Cruess Hall, #124, Davis, 95616, United States
description: “Village Homes: A Radical Plan” celebrates the 50th anniversary of an innovative west Davis neighborhood community. Curated by Professor Timothy McNeil and guest curator Adrienne McGraw, the installation showcases the vision, concepts, and development behind the project and its goals. This ground-breaking exhibition will feature original objects, archival photographs, plans and drawings, a part-built section of a typical 1970s house in Village Homes recreating a living space and demonstrating the passive solar features, and an interactive three-dimensional site plan of the neighborhood’s unique landscape. Other highlights include a 30-minute documentary film about the community.

title: Collaborative Data Science Office Hours (virtual)
description:
DataLab and colleagues from research computing and statistical service units across UC Davis offer weekly free virtual office hours during which UC Davis researchers can obtain help and advice on working with their data. During these short, just-in-time sessions we meet with faculty, students, postdoctoral scholars, and research staff from all domains across the University to discuss their research challenges.

Register in advance for the Zoom link. This helps us connect you to the right expert for your question.

In addition to DataLab, the collaborative data science office hours are supported by:

Biostatistics (Clinical and Translational Science Center, CTSC)Computational Research Data Service (College of Letters & Science)High Performance Computing (HPC) CoreResearcher Services (UC Davis Library)Stat Lab (Department of Statistics)Common office hours help topics include:

Open Source Programming (R, Python, Julia, etc.)Reproducible research computing and data scienceResponsible and ethical data scienceData gathering, organization, databases, and experimental designData analysis, statistics, and explorationData visualizationMachine learning and neural networksText mining and natural language processingGeospatial sciencesRemote and high performance computingComputational pedagogy… and more!Please note that drop-in office hours are not offered during University holidays, academic breaks and finals week. Summer schedule varies based on availability.

Note: Office hours are intended for short "just in time" research support. If you require extensive assistance we may refer you a recharge service, upcoming funding/project calls, and/or other resources. We are unable to provide support for assigned coursework (homework assignments, class projects, etc.) during office hours. If you need such help please contact your instructor or TA for assistance. If you need help with technical problems such system administration and hardware issues, check out the the UC Davis ServiceHub and/or contact your departmental IT.

title: Davis R Users' Group
location: Shields Library, 360
description:
D-RUG, our local R Users' Group, meets weekly to facilitate learning and using the R software language. R users from all domains are invited to bring their R-specific coding problems and share their experience with other members of the community. Meetup formats include everything from group discussion and mini-workshops to coworking sessions. D-RUG is run by volunteer Coo[R]dinators including UC Davis data scientists and graduate students dedicated to growing and strengthening the R community.

 

Today's session is open-forum format to foster a communal, co-working environment. Come hang out and chat, work on your own, seek and give help, and make friends.

 

D-RUG meets Thursdays in person from 10 AM to 12 PM at the DataLab Classroom (Shields Library room 360) on the UC Davis campus. 

Sign up for our mailing list to get the most up to date information, including a Zoom link to join us virtually if you can't make it in person. Learn more about D-RUG on our website.

title: NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults
location: CA
description:
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults is a free, peer-led support group for anyone between the ages 18 and 26 who has experienced symptoms of a mental health condition. Mental health conditions include anxiety disorder, depression, bipolar disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, or any condition that affects a person’s ability to lead a fulfilling life. You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend this support group; any young person concerned with their mental health and seeking support may attend. NAMI Connection for Young Adults provides resources to help young people build a support network and access help. Participants will learn together how to establish and maintain wellness. 
You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend. Anyone who is seeking mental health support may attend. 
What You’ll Gain
By sharing your experiences in a safe and confidential setting, you will experience connection, receive information about resources, and learn coping strategies. The group encourages empathy, productive discussion and a sense of community. You’ll benefit from other’s experiences, discover your inner strength, and receive support by sharing your own experiences in a non-judgmental space. 
  
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults meets on the first Wednesday of the month (in person) and on the third Wednesday of the month  (via Zoom) from 7pm-8pm.  
To register for in-person or virtual meetings, please visit: https://namiyolo.org/support-and-education/nami-yolo-county-support-groups/young-adult-connection-support-group/. 
If you have any questions, please email: friends@namiyolo.org.

It’s our show now, kid. The ghost-with-the-most comes to the stage in Beetlejuice JR., the frightfully funny musical based on the beloved motion picture turned Broadway phenomenon. Lydia Deetz is a strange and unusual teenager, still grieving the loss of her mother and obsessed with the whole “being dead thing.” Lucky for Lydia, she and her father move to a new house haunted by a recently deceased couple and Beetlejuice, a delightful demon with a real zest for life. When Lydia calls on Beetlejuice to scare away anyone with a pulse, this double-crossing specter shows his true stripes, unleashing a (Nether)world of pandemonium. 
Recommended for ages 10 & up 
  
Directed by Tea Pusey
Choreographed by Logan Sexton
Musical direction by Montana Monce

It’s our show now, kid. The ghost-with-the-most comes to the stage in Beetlejuice JR., the frightfully funny musical based on the beloved motion picture turned Broadway phenomenon. title: Collaborative Data Science Office Hours (virtual)
description:
DataLab and colleagues from research computing and statistical service units across UC Davis offer weekly free virtual office hours during which UC Davis researchers can obtain help and advice on working with their data. During these short, just-in-time sessions we meet with faculty, students, postdoctoral scholars, and research staff from all domains across the University to discuss their research challenges.

Register in advance for the Zoom link. This helps us connect you to the right expert for your question.

In addition to DataLab, the collaborative data science office hours are supported by:

Biostatistics (Clinical and Translational Science Center, CTSC)Computational Research Data Service (College of Letters & Science)High Performance Computing (HPC) CoreResearcher Services (UC Davis Library)Stat Lab (Department of Statistics)Common office hours help topics include:

Open Source Programming (R, Python, Julia, etc.)Reproducible research computing and data scienceResponsible and ethical data scienceData gathering, organization, databases, and experimental designData analysis, statistics, and explorationData visualizationMachine learning and neural networksText mining and natural language processingGeospatial sciencesRemote and high performance computingComputational pedagogy… and more!Please note that drop-in office hours are not offered during University holidays, academic breaks and finals week. Summer schedule varies based on availability.

Note: Office hours are intended for short "just in time" research support. If you require extensive assistance we may refer you a recharge service, upcoming funding/project calls, and/or other resources. We are unable to provide support for assigned coursework (homework assignments, class projects, etc.) during office hours. If you need such help please contact your instructor or TA for assistance. If you need help with technical problems such system administration and hardware issues, check out the the UC Davis ServiceHub and/or contact your departmental IT.

title: Davis R Users' Group
location: Shields Library, 360
description:
D-RUG, our local R Users' Group, meets weekly to facilitate learning and using the R software language. R users from all domains are invited to bring their R-specific coding problems and share their experience with other members of the community. Meetup formats include everything from group discussion and mini-workshops to coworking sessions. D-RUG is run by volunteer Coo[R]dinators including UC Davis data scientists and graduate students dedicated to growing and strengthening the R community.

 

Today's session is open-forum format to foster a communal, co-working environment. Come hang out and chat, work on your own, seek and give help, and make friends.

 

D-RUG meets Thursdays in person from 10 AM to 12 PM at the DataLab Classroom (Shields Library room 360) on the UC Davis campus. 

Sign up for our mailing list to get the most up to date information, including a Zoom link to join us virtually if you can't make it in person. Learn more about D-RUG on our website.

title: Creative Writing Series: Maria Kuznetsova, Fiction
location: Shields Library, 205
description:
Please join us for the next event in the 2025-2026 Creative Writing Series: a reading and Q&A featuring novelist Maria Kuznetsova!

This event is free and open to all, and will be available in person and livestreamed.

 

Maria Kuznetsova was born in Kyiv, Ukraine, and moved to the United States as a child. She is the author of the novels Oksana, Behave! and Something Unbelievable. An alum of the UC Davis master’s program in creative writing, she now lives in Auburn, Alabama, with her family, where she is an associate professor of creative writing at Auburn University. She is also the fiction editor of the Southern Humanities Review and her work appears or is forthcoming in dozens of literary journals including One Story, The Yale Review, Southern Review, Threepenny Review, and The Sun.

 

The Creative Writing Series is presented by the UC Davis Department of English, Creative Writing Master of Fine Arts Program, and the UC Davis Library.

The views expressed in this series, including descriptions and biographies, are those of the speakers and not meant to represent the views of any other person or institution.

title: Weekly Game Night at Blue Note
description:
Yolo County Board Game Gathering
Update 8/14/25:
**We're still active**, but we primarily use Discord to chat and plan events. (See group description for invite link.)

We still meet every Wednesday for board games at Blue Note Brewing Company. We'll have a handful of tables reserved near the back; just look for the pile of board games.

People usually start arriving around 5pm, and games start whenever people are ready. Blue Note closes at 9pm, and although we have a 5-10 minute grace period there, you should be in the process of final scoring by 9, or else pack it up and play again another day.

Game night is open to all ages! Blue Note is a brewery, not a bar, so there's no restriction against minors. In addition to their crafted-in-house beer selection, water and sodas are available.

You're not required to buy anything in order to join us, but we do encourage you to support local businesses. Blue Note's chef team makes a mean pizza, and there are other nearby downtown restaurants and businesses.

title: From Idea to Impact: Publish Your Work — Traditional and Alternative Options
description:
Publish Your Work: From Idea to Impact, Traditional and Alternative Options is a Zoom workshop that will give you an overview of (1) traditional publishing options, such as peer-reviewed journals, and (2) alternative publishing options, such as preprints, open methods, and open data.

Whether you’re going to be publishing for the first time or you’ve published many times in the past, join us to learn more about open research, avoiding predatory publishers, selecting an appropriate journal, and how to publish your research outputs in ways that go beyond the traditional journal article.
 

No previous experience is required. All disciplines are welcome.

Please register for the Zoom link. 

This six-workshop series is a collaboration between the library's Scholarly Communications Program, the Researcher Services department, and Blaisdell Medical Library. For any questions, please contact the Scholarly Communication Officer Michael Ladisch (mladisch@ucdavis.edu). Check our events calendar for more publishing workshops.

title: AI Literacy & Logic Workshop
description:
A free 1-day intensive workshop for College of Letters & Science majors covering foundational principles and skills for using Artificial Intelligence (AI).

 

The landscape of the academic and professional world is shifting. Whether you are an aspiring historian, a budding chemist, or a future sociologist, AI is no longer a "tech" skill—it’s a foundational literacy.

 

Developed through a collaboration between the UC Davis College of Letters & Science and DataLab, this 1-day intensive workshop is designed to unveil Artificial Intelligence. We’ve stripped away the dense math and complex coding to give you a conceptual, high-level understanding of how artificial intelligence systems work, why they matter, and how to make them work for you.

 

Why Apply to Attend?

In a single day, you will move from being a passive AI user to a critical, strategic collaborator. This training is specifically curated for L&S students to ensure you graduate with the competitive edge needed for the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. According to a LinkedIn survey report, only about a third (38%) of U.S. executives say they're currently helping their employees become AI-literate. Meanwhile, findings from a 2025 Businesswire study found that 79% of workers feel they must learn new skills and that AI literacy is the foundation for long-term competitiveness. Students who attend this all-day workshop and complete required pre- and post-workshop activities will gain foundational and applied skills in AI, and receive a certificate of completion.

 

No technical background? No problem. This workshop has no prerequisites. It is designed for thinkers, writers, and researchers across all liberal arts, social sciences and STEM disciplines.

 

The curriculum is divided into four deep-dive modules:

How AI Thinks
Understand the "brain" of the machine. We’ll explore the theories of language and cognition that allow Large Language Models (LLMs) to function, and provide a framework for evaluating their potential and limits.

How to Use AI Responsibly
AI isn't a magic wand—it’s a tool with a history and consequences. We discuss the environmental and societal impacts, how to ensure the veracity of AI results, and why reproducibility is the cornerstone of ethical tech.

How to Talk with AI
Master the art and science of prompt engineering. Learn what happens behind the scenes when you send a prompt. Develop sophisticated strategies to get the exact results you need.

How AI Talks with You
A look at the future of AI implementation. From simple chatbots to complex autonomous "Agents," learn how AI is being embedded into systems and how to evaluate its activity.

 

By the end of this one-day workshop, you will be able to:

Define "AI" and distinguish it from traditional statistics and data science.Identify whether a real-world problem is suitable for an AI solution.Develop advanced prompting strategies that produce reliable, useful results for your research and career needs.Critically interrogate your own engagement with AI and its impact on our society and environment.Practice and grow your confidence in using AI in the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. 

Who Can Apply?

All UC Davis undergraduate students with majors in the College of Letters & Science are eligible to apply. Registration priority is given to  Seniors (4th years) and students graduating in Spring or Summer 2026.  Other students in the College of Letters & Science are encouraged to register and will be admitted as space permits. Accepted learners are expected to attend all modules on their date of instruction.

 

Apply Now to Participate

This intensive, one-day workshop is offered five times during Spring Quarter 2026. Students should apply for only one date. Space is limited so don't wait to apply!

Registering for a ticket means you are applying to attend. This does not guarantee your participation. Accepted students will receive a confirmation email with the workshop location.Time: 9:00 AM – 4:30 PM with a break for a provided box lunch. Students must plan to attend all sessions, including the networking lunch.Location: In person only. The classroom location will be emailed to accepted students in advance of the workshop.Cost: This workshop is free for undergraduate students and supported by the UC Davis College of Letters & Science. 

Questions?

Reach out to Mark Foncannon at mjfoncannon@ucdavis.edu.

title: Guided Natural Wine Tasting
location: Great Bear Vineyards, Great Bear Vineyards  24800 Co Rd 101A, Davis, CA 95616
description:
Black Bicycle Farm Natural Wine Tasting in DavisSaturday, April 11 | 12:00–5:00 pm
Ready to taste wine with a little wild streak?
Step into the expressive, anything-but-ordinary world of Black Bicycle Farm at Great Bear Vineyards. This guided natural wine tasting is a deep dive into wines made from organically grown grapes, crafted with minimal intervention and maximum personality—where the vineyard, vintage, and nature itself take center stage.
 What to Expect:• A guided tasting of five distinct natural wines, each with its own story• A chance to explore orange wine, unfiltered whites, and playful carbonic reds• Insight into the philosophy, creativity, and artistry behind natural winemaking
 Featured Wines:• Squiggle 2023 – Bold, structured orange wine layered with citrus and textured depth• Orange Tipple 2023 – Perfumed with elderflower, vibrant apricot, honey, and orange notes• Clash of the Titans 2022 – Cabernet Sauvignon–Zinfandel blend with harmonious red and dark fruit• After the Fire 2020 – Cabernet Sauvignon harvested during the 2020 wildfires; smoky, spicy, and unforgettable• Carbo Cab 2019 – Playful, unfiltered Cabernet bursting with Bing cherry and candied strawberry, lifted by carbonic maceration
Set against the rustic elegance of our winery, this tasting celebrates innovation, sustainability, and letting nature do the talking—guided by people who are genuinely passionate about the craft.
Whether you’re a longtime natural wine fan or simply curious to try something new, this is a tasting that promises to be thought-provoking, delicious, and full of character.
 Tickets required. Come sip outside the box with us!

title: Raíces en Movimiento
location: International House Davis, 10 College Park, Davis, CA, 95616, United States
description:
A dynamic gathering that weaves together dance, history, and musical exploration to celebrate stories of Latin American migration. 
Part of the I-House World Tour: Roots & Routes series, Raíces en Movimiento is a multidisciplinary performance that weaves together dance, storytelling, poetry, and historical reflection to honor the sacrifices and resilience of Latin American migration. 
Through movement and voice, the performance explores themes of displacement, identity, and the search for belonging, while celebrating the ways culture continues to connect communities across borders and generations. Traditional and contemporary dances from across Latin America are paired with original narration and poetry, transforming migration stories into a shared rhythm of resilience, memory, and joy. 
More than a performance, this gathering invites community members to pause, reflect, and celebrate the power of heritage and movement as pathways for healing, connection, and cultural memory. Following the performance, guests are invited to stay, connect, and continue the conversation in community.

title: Collaborative Data Science Office Hours (virtual)
description:
DataLab and colleagues from research computing and statistical service units across UC Davis offer weekly free virtual office hours during which UC Davis researchers can obtain help and advice on working with their data. During these short, just-in-time sessions we meet with faculty, students, postdoctoral scholars, and research staff from all domains across the University to discuss their research challenges.

Register in advance for the Zoom link. This helps us connect you to the right expert for your question.

In addition to DataLab, the collaborative data science office hours are supported by:

Biostatistics (Clinical and Translational Science Center, CTSC)Computational Research Data Service (College of Letters & Science)High Performance Computing (HPC) CoreResearcher Services (UC Davis Library)Stat Lab (Department of Statistics)Common office hours help topics include:

Open Source Programming (R, Python, Julia, etc.)Reproducible research computing and data scienceResponsible and ethical data scienceData gathering, organization, databases, and experimental designData analysis, statistics, and explorationData visualizationMachine learning and neural networksText mining and natural language processingGeospatial sciencesRemote and high performance computingComputational pedagogy… and more!Please note that drop-in office hours are not offered during University holidays, academic breaks and finals week. Summer schedule varies based on availability.

Note: Office hours are intended for short "just in time" research support. If you require extensive assistance we may refer you a recharge service, upcoming funding/project calls, and/or other resources. We are unable to provide support for assigned coursework (homework assignments, class projects, etc.) during office hours. If you need such help please contact your instructor or TA for assistance. If you need help with technical problems such system administration and hardware issues, check out the the UC Davis ServiceHub and/or contact your departmental IT.

description:

Meetup formats include everything from group discussion and mini-workshops to coworking sessions. D-RUG is run by volunteer Coo[R]dinators including UC Davis data scientists and graduate students dedicated to growing and strengthening the R community.

 

Today's session is open-forum format to foster a communal, co-working environment. Come hang out and chat, work on your own, seek and give help, and make friends.

title: Covered: Faculty Author Talks by Lisa G. Materson and Corrie Decker
location: Manetti Shrem Museum
description:
Join us for an evening with UC Davis faculty authors Lisa G. Materson and Corrie Decker as part of Covered, a monthly event series celebrating recent books from the College of Letters and Science.
 

Lisa G. Materson, associate professor of history, traces civil rights activist Ruth Reynolds’ deep involvement in the movement for Puerto Rico’s independence in Radical Solidarity, offering a nuanced account of political allyship, anti-colonial struggle and cross-racial collaboration. 
 Corrie Decker, associate professor of history, examines how colonial courts and ethnographers in 20th century East Africa transformed complex rites of passage into simplified “puberty rites" in The Age of Sex: Custom, Law, and Ritual in Twentieth-Century East Africa, reshaping legal understandings of childhood, adulthood, gender and sexual maturity. 
 

Covered: Spotlighting Faculty Authors in Letters and Science is co-sponsored by the Jan Shrem and Maria Manetti Shrem Museum and the UC Davis Library.

**We're still active**, but we primarily use Discord to chat and plan events. (See group description for invite link.)

title: NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults - Virtual
location: CA
description:
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults is a free, peer-led support group for anyone between the ages 18 and 26 who has experienced symptoms of a mental health condition. Mental health conditions include anxiety disorder, depression, bipolar disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, or any condition that affects a person’s ability to lead a fulfilling life. You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend this support group; any young person concerned with their mental health and seeking support may attend. NAMI Connection for Young Adults provides resources to help young people build a support network and access help. Participants will learn together how to establish and maintain wellness. 
You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend. Anyone who is seeking mental health support may attend. 
What You’ll Gain
By sharing your experiences in a safe and confidential setting, you will experience connection, receive information about resources, and learn coping strategies. The group encourages empathy, productive discussion and a sense of community. You’ll benefit from other’s experiences, discover your inner strength, and receive support by sharing your own experiences in a non-judgmental space. 
  
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults meets on the first Wednesday of the month (in person) and on the third Wednesday of the month  (via Zoom) from 7pm-8pm.  
To register for in-person or virtual meetings, please visit: https://namiyolo.org/support-and-education/nami-yolo-county-support-groups/young-adult-connection-support-group/. 
If you have any questions, please email: friends@namiyolo.org.

title: From Idea to Impact: Publishing Your Work — Demystifying the Manuscript Submission Process
description:
Publishing research in an academic journal is a critical step in every scholar’s journey. However, it can be a very complex and intimidating landscape, especially for those who have limited experience with the process. This workshop will provide an overview of the manuscript submission process including, manuscript preparation, guidance on citing AI tools, the peer review process, and how to go about choosing copyright terms when uploading your paper.
 

No previous experience is required. All disciplines are welcome. 

Please register for the Zoom link.

This six-workshop series is a collaboration between the library's Scholarly Communications Program, the Researcher Services department, and Blaisdell Medical Library. For any questions, please contact the Scholarly Communication Officer Michael Ladisch (mladisch@ucdavis.edu). Check our events calendar for more publishing workshops.

title: Creative Writing Series: Summer Farah, Poetry
location: Shields Library, 205
description:
Please join us for the next event in the 2025-2026 Creative Writing Series: a poetry reading and Q&A featuring poet, essayist and zine-maker Summer Farah!

This event is free and open to all, and will be available in person and livestreamed.
 

Summer Farah is a Palestinian American writer, editor, and zine-maker from California. She is the author of The Hungering Years (Host Publications, 2026) and the chapbook I could die today and live again (Game Over Books, 2024). Her essay "From Witness From Speech From Image: On Etel Adnan’s The Arab Apocalypse and Theresa Hak Kyung Cha’s Dictee" received a 2025 Krause Essay Prize honorable mention. Summer is a member of the Radius of Arab American Writers and the National Book Critics Circle. She is calling on you to recommit yourself to the liberation of the Palestinian people each day.
 

This event is co-sponsored with the Department of Middle East/South Asia Studies.
 

The Creative Writing Series is presented by the UC Davis Department of English, Creative Writing Master of Fine Arts Program,
and the UC Davis Library.

The views expressed in this series, including descriptions and biographies, are those of the speakers and not meant to represent the views of any other person or institution.

title: UC Davis Picnic Day Farm-to-Fork Dinner
location: Great Bear Vineyards, Great Bear Vineyards  24800 Co Rd 101A, Davis, CA 95616
description:
Farm-to-Fork Dinner at Great Bear Vineyards in Davis — UC Davis Picnic Day Saturday, April 18 | 5:00–8:00 pm Just 3 miles from downtown Davis
After a full day of fun, traditions, and high-energy excitement at UC Davis Picnic Day, slip away from the crowds and wind down in the vineyard. Join us at Great Bear Vineyards for a relaxed Farm-to-Fork Dinner with live music, set among our organic vines and glowing in the golden light of sunset.
Whether you’re an Aggie alum, a current student, or a local looking to end the day on a high note, this is your invitation to sip, savor, and celebrate spring with great food, great wine, and even better company.
 Spring Farm-to-Fork Dinner MenuA chef-prepared feast highlighting fresh, locally sourced ingredients:• Mixed greens salad with seasonal local produce• Creamy mashed potatoes with sweet corn• Seasonal roasted vegetables• Grilled tri-tip with smoky BBQ sauce & creamed horseradish• Italian herb chicken topped with fresh tomato bruschetta• Mediterranean succotash with chickpeas, beans & garden vegetables• Bite-sized desserts for a sweet finish
 Wine Available for Purchase:• Glass of wine — $10• Bottles of Chardonnay, Rosé, or Red Blend — $38
Settle in with live music, a cozy vineyard atmosphere, and the perfect chance to slow things down after the bustle of Picnic Day. This is what spring evenings in Davis are all about.
 Great food. Great wine. Great vibes. Go Aggies!

title: Art in Action: Woven Fairy House Wall Hangings
description:
Pence Family Art Workshops
Our fairy houses are back – with a twist! Learn how to make a simple loom with sticks and twine, and weave flowers, leaves, and other natural materials to create your very own wall hanging. Decorate your woven art piece with moss, fabric butterflies, beads, and more to create the perfect fairy hideaway.

Art in Action: Woven Fairy House Wall Hangings
Sunday, April 19, 1-3 PM
$5 Members, $10 Non-Members (per person, all materials included)

**How it works:**
Art in Action engages kids in art projects that are fun, creative, and collaborative with their family members. This is a drop-in program for ages 5-12. No registration in advance is required, but space and supplies are limited to the first 25 participants and are available on a first come, first served basis.

When you arrive, pay with cash or card at the front desk. A parent or adult caregiver is required to stay, but only has to pay for the project for themselves if they are also doing art. Stay as long or as short as you like over the two hour period.

Become a Member starting at $70/year and save! If you join the Pence Gallery when you attend this workshop, you can enjoy Member pricing for Art in Action, plus receive a $5 gift certificate to our gift shop as a thank you. Membership supports art for all ages and makes creative moments like this possible—jump in and be part of it.

We have a different Art in Action family workshop every month! Learn more at [https://pencegallery.org/art-in-action/](https://pencegallery.org/art-in-action/).

This unique monthly program is generously sponsored and made possible by Mary Stephens DeWall and Les DeWall.

title: Collaborative Data Science Office Hours (virtual)
description:
DataLab and colleagues from research computing and statistical service units across UC Davis offer weekly free virtual office hours during which UC Davis researchers can obtain help and advice on working with their data. During these short, just-in-time sessions we meet with faculty, students, postdoctoral scholars, and research staff from all domains across the University to discuss their research challenges.

Register in advance for the Zoom link. This helps us connect you to the right expert for your question.

In addition to DataLab, the collaborative data science office hours are supported by:

Biostatistics (Clinical and Translational Science Center, CTSC)Computational Research Data Service (College of Letters & Science)High Performance Computing (HPC) CoreResearcher Services (UC Davis Library)Stat Lab (Department of Statistics)Common office hours help topics include:

Open Source Programming (R, Python, Julia, etc.)Reproducible research computing and data scienceResponsible and ethical data scienceData gathering, organization, databases, and experimental designData analysis, statistics, and explorationData visualizationMachine learning and neural networksText mining and natural language processingGeospatial sciencesRemote and high performance computingComputational pedagogy… and more!Please note that drop-in office hours are not offered during University holidays, academic breaks and finals week. Summer schedule varies based on availability.

Note: Office hours are intended for short "just in time" research support. If you require extensive assistance we may refer you a recharge service, upcoming funding/project calls, and/or other resources. We are unable to provide support for assigned coursework (homework assignments, class projects, etc.) during office hours. If you need such help please contact your instructor or TA for assistance. If you need help with technical problems such system administration and hardware issues, check out the the UC Davis ServiceHub and/or contact your departmental IT.

title: Davis R Users' Group

description:
D-RUG, our local R Users' Group, meets weekly to facilitate learning and using the R software language. R users from all domains are invited to bring their R-specific coding problems and share their experience with other members of the community. Meetup formats include everything from group discussion and mini-workshops to coworking sessions. D-RUG is run by volunteer Coo[R]dinators including UC Davis data scientists and graduate students dedicated to growing and strengthening the R community.

 

Today's session is open-forum format to foster a communal, co-working environment. Come hang out and chat, work on your own, seek and give help, and make friends.

 

D-RUG meets Thursdays in person from 10 AM to 12 PM at the DataLab Classroom (Shields Library room 360) on the UC Davis campus. 

Sign up for our mailing list to get the most up to date information, including a Zoom link to join us virtually if you can't make it in person. Learn more about D-RUG on our website.

location: Central Park, 301 C St., Davis, CA, 95616, United States
description:
The beloved Picnic in the Park season is back at the Davis Farmers Market starting Wednesday, April 1, 2026! 
Every Wednesday evening through August 26, the market expands to 4:00 – 8:00 PM, turning Central Park into the perfect spot for a relaxed picnic dinner. Shop fresh produce, artisan goods, and a wide variety of prepared foods from your favorite vendors. Enjoy local craft beer, wine, and brand new this year – mead! 
Each week features live music and children’s activities, creating a festive, family-friendly atmosphere. Bring blankets, lawn chairs, and reusable bags. It’s the ideal way to unwind, connect with neighbors, and support local farmers and food makers. 
This year is extra special as the Davis Farmers Market celebrates its 50th Anniversary! Don’t miss the big anniversary celebration on Saturday, August 1, 2026. 
New in 2026: The Market Memories Cookbook (presale now at davis-farmers-market).

Buckhorn Food Truck, The Hotdogger, Dhillon Farm, Davis Bread & Desserts, Greek Table, Kettle Pop & more

Kids’ activities
Local beer, wine & mead

Free to attend – everyone is welcome!

title: From Idea to Impact: Publishing Your Work — Open Access Publishing: Mandates, Policies, Support
description:
Maximize your research impact and global reach by publishing Open Access. In this workshop we will show you how to use UC’s transformative agreements and other library initiatives to have the Library cover your publishing fees, ensuring your work is freely available from day one. We will briefly address staying compliant with federal public access mandates, and provide a short overview of the UC Open Access Policies.
 

No previous experience is required. All disciplines are welcome. 

Please register for the Zoom link.

This six-workshop series is a collaboration between the library's Scholarly Communications Program, the Researcher Services department, and Blaisdell Medical Library. For any questions, please contact the Scholarly Communication Officer Michael Ladisch (mladisch@ucdavis.edu). Check our events calendar for more publishing workshops.

title: Collaborative Data Science Office Hours (virtual)
description:
DataLab and colleagues from research computing and statistical service units across UC Davis offer weekly free virtual office hours during which UC Davis researchers can obtain help and advice on working with their data. During these short, just-in-time sessions we meet with faculty, students, postdoctoral scholars, and research staff from all domains across the University to discuss their research challenges.

Register in advance for the Zoom link. This helps us connect you to the right expert for your question.

In addition to DataLab, the collaborative data science office hours are supported by:

Biostatistics (Clinical and Translational Science Center, CTSC)Computational Research Data Service (College of Letters & Science)High Performance Computing (HPC) CoreResearcher Services (UC Davis Library)Stat Lab (Department of Statistics)Common office hours help topics include:

Open Source Programming (R, Python, Julia, etc.)Reproducible research computing and data scienceResponsible and ethical data scienceData gathering, organization, databases, and experimental designData analysis, statistics, and explorationData visualizationMachine learning and neural networksText mining and natural language processingGeospatial sciencesRemote and high performance computingComputational pedagogy… and more!Please note that drop-in office hours are not offered during University holidays, academic breaks and finals week. Summer schedule varies based on availability.

Note: Office hours are intended for short "just in time" research support. If you require extensive assistance we may refer you a recharge service, upcoming funding/project calls, and/or other resources. We are unable to provide support for assigned coursework (homework assignments, class projects, etc.) during office hours. If you need such help please contact your instructor or TA for assistance. description:

title: Weekly Game Night at Blue Note
description:
Yolo County Board Game Gathering
Update 8/14/25:
**We're still active**, but we primarily use Discord to chat and plan events. (See group description for invite link.)

We still meet every Wednesday for board games at Blue Note Brewing Company. We'll have a handful of tables reserved near the back; just look for the pile of board games.

People usually start arriving around 5pm, and games start whenever people are ready. Blue Note closes at 9pm, and although we have a 5-10 minute grace period there, you should be in the process of final scoring by 9, or else pack it up and play again another day.

Game night is open to all ages! Blue Note is a brewery, not a bar, so there's no restriction against minors. In addition to their crafted-in-house beer selection, water and sodas are available.

You're not required to buy anything in order to join us, but we do encourage you to support local businesses. Blue Note's chef team makes a mean pizza, and there are other nearby downtown restaurants and businesses.

title: From Idea to Impact: Publishing Your Work — Navigating Data Management for Publication
description:
This workshop is targeted toward scholars, researchers, and graduate students who are interested in the role of data management during the publication process. Managing your data effectively is a crucial part of research and scholarly communication. This session will provide foundational skills for handling data throughout your project's lifecycle, from planning for funder requirements to ensuring long-term reproducibility.
 

After this workshop, learners should be able to:
 

Understand and navigate funder requirements for data management and sharing.Use the Data Management Planning (DMP) Tool to organize research projects.Apply fundamental data management strategies during the active execution phase of research.Protect sensitive or personal data if collected during researchDistinguish between working data storage and open data repositories (platforms used for sharing and archiving final data).Locate the appropriate data repository for your specific discipline or project.Explain the broader importance of data reproducibility in the research ecosystem.Identify campus resources available for data support, including the DataLab, Research Data Management (RDM) Team, and the Clinical and Translational Science Center (CTSC).
No previous experience is required. All disciplines are welcome.


Please register for the Zoom link.

This six-workshop series is a collaboration between the library's Scholarly Communications Program, the Researcher Services department, and Blaisdell Medical Library. For any questions, please contact the Scholarly Communication Officer Michael Ladisch (mladisch@ucdavis.edu). Check our events calendar for more publishing workshops.

title: AI Literacy & Logic Workshop
description:
A free 1-day intensive workshop for College of Letters & Science majors covering foundational principles and skills for using Artificial Intelligence (AI).

 

The landscape of the academic and professional world is shifting. Whether you are an aspiring historian, a budding chemist, or a future sociologist, AI is no longer a "tech" skill—it’s a foundational literacy.

 

Developed through a collaboration between the UC Davis College of Letters & Science and DataLab, this 1-day intensive workshop is designed to unveil Artificial Intelligence. We’ve stripped away the dense math and complex coding to give you a conceptual, high-level understanding of how artificial intelligence systems work, why they matter, and how to make them work for you.

 

Why Apply to Attend?

In a single day, you will move from being a passive AI user to a critical, strategic collaborator. This training is specifically curated for L&S students to ensure you graduate with the competitive edge needed for the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. According to a LinkedIn survey report, only about a third (38%) of U.S. executives say they're currently helping their employees become AI-literate. Meanwhile, findings from a 2025 Businesswire study found that 79% of workers feel they must learn new skills and that AI literacy is the foundation for long-term competitiveness. Students who attend this all-day workshop and complete required pre- and post-workshop activities will gain foundational and applied skills in AI, and receive a certificate of completion.

 

No technical background? No problem. This workshop has no prerequisites. It is designed for thinkers, writers, and researchers across all liberal arts, social sciences and STEM disciplines.

 

The curriculum is divided into four deep-dive modules:

How AI Thinks
Understand the "brain" of the machine. We’ll explore the theories of language and cognition that allow Large Language Models (LLMs) to function, and provide a framework for evaluating their potential and limits.

How to Use AI Responsibly
AI isn't a magic wand—it’s a tool with a history and consequences. We discuss the environmental and societal impacts, how to ensure the veracity of AI results, and why reproducibility is the cornerstone of ethical tech.

How to Talk with AI
Master the art and science of prompt engineering. Learn what happens behind the scenes when you send a prompt. Develop sophisticated strategies to get the exact results you need.

How AI Talks with You
A look at the future of AI implementation. From simple chatbots to complex autonomous "Agents," learn how AI is being embedded into systems and how to evaluate its activity.

 

By the end of this one-day workshop, you will be able to:

Define "AI" and distinguish it from traditional statistics and data science.Identify whether a real-world problem is suitable for an AI solution.Develop advanced prompting strategies that produce reliable, useful results for your research and career needs.Critically interrogate your own engagement with AI and its impact on our society and environment.Practice and grow your confidence in using AI in the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. 

Who Can Apply?

All UC Davis undergraduate students with majors in the College of Letters & Science are eligible to apply. Registration priority is given to  Seniors (4th years) and students graduating in Spring or Summer 2026.  Other students in the College of Letters & Science are encouraged to register and will be admitted as space permits. Accepted learners are expected to attend all modules on their date of instruction.

 

Apply Now to Participate

This intensive, one-day workshop is offered five times during Spring Quarter 2026. Students should apply for only one date. Space is limited so don't wait to apply!

Registering for a ticket means you are applying to attend. This does not guarantee your participation. Accepted students will receive a confirmation email with the workshop location.Time: 9:00 AM – 4:30 PM with a break for a provided box lunch. Students must plan to attend all sessions, including the networking lunch.Location: In person only. The classroom location will be emailed to accepted students in advance of the workshop.Cost: This workshop is free for undergraduate students and supported by the UC Davis College of Letters & Science. 

Questions?

Reach out to Mark Foncannon at mjfoncannon@ucdavis.edu.

title: Collaborative Data Science Office Hours (virtual)
description:
DataLab and colleagues from research computing and statistical service units across UC Davis offer weekly free virtual office hours during which UC Davis researchers can obtain help and advice on working with their data. During these short, just-in-time sessions we meet with faculty, students, postdoctoral scholars, and research staff from all domains across the University to discuss their research challenges.

Register in advance for the Zoom link. This helps us connect you to the right expert for your question.

In addition to DataLab, the collaborative data science office hours are supported by:

Biostatistics (Clinical and Translational Science Center, CTSC)Computational Research Data Service (College of Letters & Science)High Performance Computing (HPC) CoreResearcher Services (UC Davis Library)Stat Lab (Department of Statistics)Common office hours help topics include:

Open Source Programming (R, Python, Julia, etc.)Reproducible research computing and data scienceResponsible and ethical data scienceData gathering, organization, databases, and experimental designData analysis, statistics, and explorationData visualizationMachine learning and neural networksText mining and natural language processingGeospatial sciencesRemote and high performance computingComputational pedagogy… and more!Please note that drop-in office hours are not offered during University holidays, academic breaks and finals week. Summer schedule varies based on availability.

Note: Office hours are intended for short "just in time" research support. If you require extensive assistance we may refer you a recharge service, upcoming funding/project calls, and/or other resources. We are unable to provide support for assigned coursework (homework assignments, class projects, etc.) during office hours. If you need such help please contact your instructor or TA for assistance. If you need help with technical problems such system administration and hardware issues, check out the the UC Davis ServiceHub and/or contact your departmental IT.

title: Davis R Users' Group
location: Shields Library, 360
description:
D-RUG, our local R Users' Group, meets weekly to facilitate learning and using the R software language. R users from all domains are invited to bring their R-specific coding problems and share their experience with other members of the community. Meetup formats include everything from group discussion and mini-workshops to coworking sessions. D-RUG is run by volunteer Coo[R]dinators including UC Davis data scientists and graduate students dedicated to growing and strengthening the R community.

 

Today's session is open-forum format to foster a communal, co-working environment. Come hang out and chat, work on your own, seek and give help, and make friends.

 

D-RUG meets Thursdays in person from 10 AM to 12 PM at the DataLab Classroom (Shields Library room 360) on the UC Davis campus. 

Sign up for our mailing list to get the most up to date information, including a Zoom link to join us virtually if you can't make it in person. Learn more about D-RUG on our website.

title: NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults
location: CA
description:
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults is a free, peer-led support group for anyone between the ages 18 and 26 who has experienced symptoms of a mental health condition. Mental health conditions include anxiety disorder, depression, bipolar disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, or any condition that affects a person’s ability to lead a fulfilling life. You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend this support group; any young person concerned with their mental health and seeking support may attend. NAMI Connection for Young Adults provides resources to help young people build a support network and access help. Participants will learn together how to establish and maintain wellness. 
You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend. Anyone who is seeking mental health support may attend. 
What You’ll Gain
By sharing your experiences in a safe and confidential setting, you will experience connection, receive information about resources, and learn coping strategies. The group encourages empathy, productive discussion and a sense of community. You’ll benefit from other’s experiences, discover your inner strength, and receive support by sharing your own experiences in a non-judgmental space. 
  
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults meets on the first Wednesday of the month (in person) and on the third Wednesday of the month  (via Zoom) from 7pm-8pm.  
To register for in-person or virtual meetings, please visit: https://namiyolo.org/support-and-education/nami-yolo-county-support-groups/young-adult-connection-support-group/. 
If you have any questions, please email: friends@namiyolo.org.

title: From Idea to Impact: Publishing Your Work — Copyright and Other Legal Issues
description:
This workshop provides information on owning your work, from understanding how to retain copyright in your first publications to navigating the fine print of author agreements. We will explore how to safely incorporate third-party copyrighted material into your publication and how to strategically use Creative Commons licenses to dictate how others cite and share your findings.
 

No previous experience is required. All disciplines are welcome.


Please register for the Zoom link.

This six-workshop series is a collaboration between the library's Scholarly Communications Program, the Researcher Services department, and Blaisdell Medical Library. For any questions, please contact the Scholarly Communication Officer Michael Ladisch (mladisch@ucdavis.edu). Check our events calendar for more publishing workshops.

title: AI Literacy & Logic Workshop
description:
A free 1-day intensive workshop for College of Letters & Science majors covering foundational principles and skills for using Artificial Intelligence (AI).

 

The landscape of the academic and professional world is shifting. Whether you are an aspiring historian, a budding chemist, or a future sociologist, AI is no longer a "tech" skill—it’s a foundational literacy.

 

Developed through a collaboration between the UC Davis College of Letters & Science and DataLab, this 1-day intensive workshop is designed to unveil Artificial Intelligence. We’ve stripped away the dense math and complex coding to give you a conceptual, high-level understanding of how artificial intelligence systems work, why they matter, and how to make them work for you.

 

Why Apply to Attend?

In a single day, you will move from being a passive AI user to a critical, strategic collaborator. This training is specifically curated for L&S students to ensure you graduate with the competitive edge needed for the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. According to a LinkedIn survey report, only about a third (38%) of U.S. executives say they're currently helping their employees become AI-literate. Meanwhile, findings from a 2025 Businesswire study found that 79% of workers feel they must learn new skills and that AI literacy is the foundation for long-term competitiveness. Students who attend this all-day workshop and complete required pre- and post-workshop activities will gain foundational and applied skills in AI, and receive a certificate of completion.

 

No technical background? No problem. This workshop has no prerequisites. It is designed for thinkers, writers, and researchers across all liberal arts, social sciences and STEM disciplines.

 

The curriculum is divided into four deep-dive modules:

How AI Thinks
Understand the "brain" of the machine. We’ll explore the theories of language and cognition that allow Large Language Models (LLMs) to function, and provide a framework for evaluating their potential and limits.

How to Use AI Responsibly
AI isn't a magic wand—it’s a tool with a history and consequences. We discuss the environmental and societal impacts, how to ensure the veracity of AI results, and why reproducibility is the cornerstone of ethical tech.

How to Talk with AI
Master the art and science of prompt engineering. Learn what happens behind the scenes when you send a prompt. Develop sophisticated strategies to get the exact results you need.

How AI Talks with You
A look at the future of AI implementation. From simple chatbots to complex autonomous "Agents," learn how AI is being embedded into systems and how to evaluate its activity.

 

By the end of this one-day workshop, you will be able to:

Define "AI" and distinguish it from traditional statistics and data science.Identify whether a real-world problem is suitable for an AI solution.Develop advanced prompting strategies that produce reliable, useful results for your research and career needs.Critically interrogate your own engagement with AI and its impact on our society and environment.Practice and grow your confidence in using AI in the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. 

Who Can Apply?

All UC Davis undergraduate students with majors in the College of Letters & Science are eligible to apply. Registration priority is given to  Seniors (4th years) and students graduating in Spring or Summer 2026.  Other students in the College of Letters & Science are encouraged to register and will be admitted as space permits. Accepted learners are expected to attend all modules on their date of instruction.

 

Apply Now to Participate

This intensive, one-day workshop is offered five times during Spring Quarter 2026. Students should apply for only one date. Space is limited so don't wait to apply!

Registering for a ticket means you are applying to attend. This does not guarantee your participation. Accepted students will receive a confirmation email with the workshop location.Time: 9:00 AM – 4:30 PM with a break for a provided box lunch. Students must plan to attend all sessions, including the networking lunch.Location: In person only. The classroom location will be emailed to accepted students in advance of the workshop.Cost: This workshop is free for undergraduate students and supported by the UC Davis College of Letters & Science.

title: Grad Fest
description:
Celebratory Grad Fest will be a free campus wide festival open to all students, featuring food, speakers, student grad acknowledgment, music and dance performances, and local vendors/Bazaar. The event provides a joyful space for this year's graduates to celebrate their achievements with classmates, friends, and family. Graduates will have the opportunity to submit their names for public shout outs during the event, giving them well deserved recognition. There will also be a photo station; with a graduation backdrop, a "I did it!" Sign, and Family photo corner.
We are also planning on having a cap-decorating station. 
This fest not only honors individual accomplishments but also highlights the value of education and the hard work it takes to earn a degree. This event will blend culture, performance, and social bonding. It centers global awareness by connecting SOF students in Afghanistan with local graduates here. This in turn brings families, cultures, and communities together. It promotes cultural engagement and inclusivity. Educational programming through storytelling, performers, community resources, and cultural vendors. In turn, this will help break down stereotypes and bring people together through shared understanding.
What we're doing with this ceremony is building a bridge between communities that usually feel worlds apart. We're creating a moment where students from completely different backgrounds can be celebrated together, and that shared recognition helps grow understanding, empathy, and acceptance. By honoring students from all communities who have achieved success in higher education, we're also highlighting the importance of inclusivity, gender equality, and the right to education. That matters even more when we think about the girls in Afghanistan who would give anything just to go to school and who were still able to pursue their education despite the Taliban trying to stop them. These young women have worked incredibly hard to graduate, and hosting a ceremony for them alongside UC Davis students not only celebrates achievement but shows the power of connection, support, and global solidarity. It's a reminder that education shouldn't be limited by gender or geography, and that celebrating each other's success is itself a form of resistance.

title: Collaborative Data Science Office Hours (virtual)
description:
DataLab and colleagues from research computing and statistical service units across UC Davis offer weekly free virtual office hours during which UC Davis researchers can obtain help and advice on working with their data. During these short, just-in-time sessions we meet with faculty, students, postdoctoral scholars, and research staff from all domains across the University to discuss their research challenges.

Register in advance for the Zoom link. This helps us connect you to the right expert for your question.

In addition to DataLab, the collaborative data science office hours are supported by:

Biostatistics (Clinical and Translational Science Center, CTSC)Computational Research Data Service (College of Letters & Science)High Performance Computing (HPC) CoreResearcher Services (UC Davis Library)Stat Lab (Department of Statistics)Common office hours help topics include:

Open Source Programming (R, Python, Julia, etc.)Reproducible research computing and data scienceResponsible and ethical data scienceData gathering, organization, databases, and experimental designData analysis, statistics, and explorationData visualizationMachine learning and neural networksText mining and natural language processingGeospatial sciencesRemote and high performance computingComputational pedagogy… and more!Please note that drop-in office hours are not offered during University holidays, academic breaks and finals week. Summer schedule varies based on availability.

Note: Office hours are intended for short "just in time" research support. If you require extensive assistance we may refer you a recharge service, upcoming funding/project calls, and/or other resources. We are unable to provide support for assigned coursework (homework assignments, class projects, etc.) during office hours. If you need such help please contact your instructor or TA for assistance. If you need help with technical problems such system administration and hardware issues, check out the the UC Davis ServiceHub and/or contact your departmental IT.

title: Davis R Users' Group
location: Shields Library, 360
description:
D-RUG, our local R Users' Group, meets weekly to facilitate learning and using the R software language. R users from all domains are invited to bring their R-specific coding problems and share their experience with other members of the community. Meetup formats include everything from group discussion and mini-workshops to coworking sessions. D-RUG is run by volunteer Coo[R]dinators including UC Davis data scientists and graduate students dedicated to growing and strengthening the R community.

 

Today's session is open-forum format to foster a communal, co-working environment. Come hang out and chat, work on your own, seek and give help, and make friends.

 

D-RUG meets Thursdays in person from 10 AM to 12 PM at the DataLab Classroom (Shields Library room 360) on the UC Davis campus. 

Sign up for our mailing list to get the most up to date information, including a Zoom link to join us virtually if you can't make it in person. Learn more about D-RUG on our website.

description:
Join us for an evening with UC Davis faculty authors Claire Goldstein and Michael Dylan Foster as part of Covered, a monthly event series celebrating recent books from the College of Letters and Science.
 

Claire Goldstein, professor of French and Italian, discusses In the Sun King’s Cosmos, her engaging study of how comets shaped scientific, political and cultural imagination in 17th century France. 
 Michael Dylan Foster, professor of East Asian Languages and Cultures, presents the expanded edition of The Book of Yokai, exploring the folklore, history and ongoing cultural life of Japan’s supernatural beings. 



 

Covered: Spotlighting Faculty Authors in Letters and Science is co-sponsored by the Jan Shrem and Maria Manetti Shrem Museum and the UC Davis Library.

title: Weekly Game Night at Blue Note
description:
Yolo County Board Game Gathering
Update 8/14/25:
**We're still active**, but we primarily use Discord to chat and plan events. (See group description for invite link.)

We still meet every Wednesday for board games at Blue Note Brewing Company. We'll have a handful of tables reserved near the back; just look for the pile of board games.

People usually start arriving around 5pm, and games start whenever people are ready. Blue Note closes at 9pm, and although we have a 5-10 minute grace period there, you should be in the process of final scoring by 9, or else pack it up and play again another day.

Game night is open to all ages! Blue Note is a brewery, not a bar, so there's no restriction against minors. In addition to their crafted-in-house beer selection, water and sodas are available.

You're not required to buy anything in order to join us, but we do encourage you to support local businesses. Blue Note's chef team makes a mean pizza, and there are other nearby downtown restaurants and businesses.

title: AI Literacy & Logic Workshop
description:
A free 1-day intensive workshop for College of Letters & Science majors covering foundational principles and skills for using Artificial Intelligence (AI).

 

The landscape of the academic and professional world is shifting. Whether you are an aspiring historian, a budding chemist, or a future sociologist, AI is no longer a "tech" skill—it’s a foundational literacy.

 

Developed through a collaboration between the UC Davis College of Letters & Science and DataLab, this 1-day intensive workshop is designed to unveil Artificial Intelligence. We’ve stripped away the dense math and complex coding to give you a conceptual, high-level understanding of how artificial intelligence systems work, why they matter, and how to make them work for you.

 

Why Apply to Attend?

In a single day, you will move from being a passive AI user to a critical, strategic collaborator. This training is specifically curated for L&S students to ensure you graduate with the competitive edge needed for the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. According to a LinkedIn survey report, only about a third (38%) of U.S. executives say they're currently helping their employees become AI-literate. Meanwhile, findings from a 2025 Businesswire study found that 79% of workers feel they must learn new skills and that AI literacy is the foundation for long-term competitiveness. Students who attend this all-day workshop and complete required pre- and post-workshop activities will gain foundational and applied skills in AI, and receive a certificate of completion.

 

No technical background? No problem. This workshop has no prerequisites. It is designed for thinkers, writers, and researchers across all liberal arts, social sciences and STEM disciplines.

 

The curriculum is divided into four deep-dive modules:

How AI Thinks
Understand the "brain" of the machine. We’ll explore the theories of language and cognition that allow Large Language Models (LLMs) to function, and provide a framework for evaluating their potential and limits.

How to Use AI Responsibly
AI isn't a magic wand—it’s a tool with a history and consequences. We discuss the environmental and societal impacts, how to ensure the veracity of AI results, and why reproducibility is the cornerstone of ethical tech.

How to Talk with AI
Master the art and science of prompt engineering. Learn what happens behind the scenes when you send a prompt. Develop sophisticated strategies to get the exact results you need.

How AI Talks with You
A look at the future of AI implementation. From simple chatbots to complex autonomous "Agents," learn how AI is being embedded into systems and how to evaluate its activity.

 

By the end of this one-day workshop, you will be able to:

Define "AI" and distinguish it from traditional statistics and data science.Identify whether a real-world problem is suitable for an AI solution.Develop advanced prompting strategies that produce reliable, useful results for your research and career needs.Critically interrogate your own engagement with AI and its impact on our society and environment.Practice and grow your confidence in using AI in the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. 

Who Can Apply?

All UC Davis undergraduate students with majors in the College of Letters & Science are eligible to apply. Registration priority is given to  Seniors (4th years) and students graduating in Spring or Summer 2026.  Other students in the College of Letters & Science are encouraged to register and will be admitted as space permits. Accepted learners are expected to attend all modules on their date of instruction.

 

Apply Now to Participate

This intensive, one-day workshop is offered five times during Spring Quarter 2026. Students should apply for only one date. Space is limited so don't wait to apply!

Registering for a ticket means you are applying to attend. This does not guarantee your participation. Accepted students will receive a confirmation email with the workshop location.Time: 9:00 AM – 4:30 PM with a break for a provided box lunch. Students must plan to attend all sessions, including the networking lunch.Location: In person only. The classroom location will be emailed to accepted students in advance of the workshop.Cost: This workshop is free for undergraduate students and supported by the UC Davis College of Letters & Science. 

Questions?

Reach out to Mark Foncannon at mjfoncannon@ucdavis.edu.

title: NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults - Virtual
location: CA
description:
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults is a free, peer-led support group for anyone between the ages 18 and 26 who has experienced symptoms of a mental health condition. Mental health conditions include anxiety disorder, depression, bipolar disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, or any condition that affects a person’s ability to lead a fulfilling life. You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend this support group; any young person concerned with their mental health and seeking support may attend. NAMI Connection for Young Adults provides resources to help young people build a support network and access help. Participants will learn together how to establish and maintain wellness. 
You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend. Anyone who is seeking mental health support may attend. 
What You’ll Gain
By sharing your experiences in a safe and confidential setting, you will experience connection, receive information about resources, and learn coping strategies. The group encourages empathy, productive discussion and a sense of community. You’ll benefit from other’s experiences, discover your inner strength, and receive support by sharing your own experiences in a non-judgmental space. 
  
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults meets on the first Wednesday of the month (in person) and on the third Wednesday of the month  (via Zoom) from 7pm-8pm.  
To register for in-person or virtual meetings, please visit: https://namiyolo.org/support-and-education/nami-yolo-county-support-groups/young-adult-connection-support-group/. 
If you have any questions, please email: friends@namiyolo.org.

title: From Idea to Impact: Publishing Your Work — Researcher IDs & Tracking Your Research Impact
description:
Build your digital research identity and ensure your work gets the credit it deserves with this focused workshop on persistent identifiers and impact tracking. This session provides a practical roadmap for managing ORCID, Scopus ID, and Web of Science profiles to eliminate author ambiguity.
 

We will also demonstrate how to strategically monitor your publication impact using tools like Scopus and Google Scholar, moving beyond simple citation counts to understand your global reach and field-weighted influence.
 

No previous experience is required. All disciplines are welcome.


Please register for the Zoom link.

This six-workshop series is a collaboration between the library's Scholarly Communications Program, the Researcher Services department, and Blaisdell Medical Library. For any questions, please contact the Scholarly Communication Officer Michael Ladisch (mladisch@ucdavis.edu). Check our events calendar for more publishing workshops.

title: AI Literacy & Logic Workshop
description:
A free 1-day intensive workshop for College of Letters & Science majors covering foundational principles and skills for using Artificial Intelligence (AI).

 

The landscape of the academic and professional world is shifting. Whether you are an aspiring historian, a budding chemist, or a future sociologist, AI is no longer a "tech" skill—it’s a foundational literacy.

 

Developed through a collaboration between the UC Davis College of Letters & Science and DataLab, this 1-day intensive workshop is designed to unveil Artificial Intelligence. We’ve stripped away the dense math and complex coding to give you a conceptual, high-level understanding of how artificial intelligence systems work, why they matter, and how to make them work for you.

 

Why Apply to Attend?

In a single day, you will move from being a passive AI user to a critical, strategic collaborator. This training is specifically curated for L&S students to ensure you graduate with the competitive edge needed for the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. According to a LinkedIn survey report, only about a third (38%) of U.S. executives say they're currently helping their employees become AI-literate. Meanwhile, findings from a 2025 Businesswire study found that 79% of workers feel they must learn new skills and that AI literacy is the foundation for long-term competitiveness. Students who attend this all-day workshop and complete required pre- and post-workshop activities will gain foundational and applied skills in AI, and receive a certificate of completion.

 

No technical background? No problem. This workshop has no prerequisites. It is designed for thinkers, writers, and researchers across all liberal arts, social sciences and STEM disciplines.

 

The curriculum is divided into four deep-dive modules:

How AI Thinks
Understand the "brain" of the machine. We’ll explore the theories of language and cognition that allow Large Language Models (LLMs) to function, and provide a framework for evaluating their potential and limits.

How to Use AI Responsibly
AI isn't a magic wand—it’s a tool with a history and consequences. We discuss the environmental and societal impacts, how to ensure the veracity of AI results, and why reproducibility is the cornerstone of ethical tech.

How to Talk with AI
Master the art and science of prompt engineering. Learn what happens behind the scenes when you send a prompt. Develop sophisticated strategies to get the exact results you need.

How AI Talks with You
A look at the future of AI implementation. From simple chatbots to complex autonomous "Agents," learn how AI is being embedded into systems and how to evaluate its activity.

 

By the end of this one-day workshop, you will be able to:

Define "AI" and distinguish it from traditional statistics and data science.Identify whether a real-world problem is suitable for an AI solution.Develop advanced prompting strategies that produce reliable, useful results for your research and career needs.Critically interrogate your own engagement with AI and its impact on our society and environment.Practice and grow your confidence in using AI in the rapidly evolving, AI-driven workplace. 

Who Can Apply?

All UC Davis undergraduate students with majors in the College of Letters & Science are eligible to apply. Registration priority is given to  Seniors (4th years) and students graduating in Spring or Summer 2026.  Other students in the College of Letters & Science are encouraged to register and will be admitted as space permits. Accepted learners are expected to attend all modules on their date of instruction.

 

Apply Now to Participate

This intensive, one-day workshop is offered five times during Spring Quarter 2026. Students should apply for only one date. Space is limited so don't wait to apply!

Registering for a ticket means you are applying to attend. This does not guarantee your participation. Accepted students will receive a confirmation email with the workshop location.Time: 9:00 AM – 4:30 PM with a break for a provided box lunch. Students must plan to attend all sessions, including the networking lunch.Location: In person only. The classroom location will be emailed to accepted students in advance of the workshop.Cost: This workshop is free for undergraduate students and supported by the UC Davis College of Letters & Science. 

Questions?

Reach out to Mark Foncannon at mjfoncannon@ucdavis.edu.

title: Blood on the Clocktower at Blue Note
description:
Yolo County Board Game Gathering
The first Wednesday of every month, we play _Blood on the Clocktower_, a social deduction game in the vein of Werewolf/Mafia/The Resistance, with a few twists!

We'll have a circle of chairs reserved inside Blue Note, near the back, with a Yolo County Board Game Gathering sign on the table. Just say hello, and we'll show you some games, pull some players together, and get to having fun!

We also usually have a few tables of players playing other board games, if social deduction isn't your bag!

Get a feel for Blood on the Clocktower here: https://youtu.be/4sfa8_kNxsQ

title: NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults
location: CA
description:
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults is a free, peer-led support group for anyone between the ages 18 and 26 who has experienced symptoms of a mental health condition. Mental health conditions include anxiety disorder, depression, bipolar disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, or any condition that affects a person’s ability to lead a fulfilling life. You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend this support group; any young person concerned with their mental health and seeking support may attend. NAMI Connection for Young Adults provides resources to help young people build a support network and access help. Participants will learn together how to establish and maintain wellness. 
You do not need a formal diagnosis to attend. Anyone who is seeking mental health support may attend. 
What You’ll Gain
By sharing your experiences in a safe and confidential setting, you will experience connection, receive information about resources, and learn coping strategies. The group encourages empathy, productive discussion and a sense of community. You’ll benefit from other’s experiences, discover your inner strength, and receive support by sharing your own experiences in a non-judgmental space. 
  
NAMI Connection Support Group for Young Adults meets on the first Wednesday of the month (in person) and on the third Wednesday of the month  (via Zoom) from 7pm-8pm.  
To register for in-person or virtual meetings, please visit: https://namiyolo.org/support-and-education/nami-yolo-county-support-groups/young-adult-connection-support-group/. 
If you have any questions, please email: friends@namiyolo.org.

title: Weekly Game Night at Blue Note
description:
Yolo County Board Game Gathering
Update 8/14/25:
**We're still active**, but we primarily use Discord to chat and plan events. (See group description for invite link.)

We still meet every Wednesday for board games at Blue Note Brewing Company. We'll have a handful of tables reserved near the back; just look for the pile of board games.

People usually start arriving around 5pm, and games start whenever people are ready. Blue Note closes at 9pm, and although we have a 5-10 minute grace period there, you should be in the process of final scoring by 9, or else pack it up and play again another day.

Game night is open to all ages! Blue Note is a brewery, not a bar, so there's no restriction against minors. In addition to their crafted-in-house beer selection, water and sodas are available.

You're not required to buy anything in order to join us, but we do encourage you to support local businesses. Blue Note's chef team makes a mean pizza, and there are other nearby downtown restaurants and businesses.
